exemplar.title: How does cognitive psychology theories explain the enjoyment and suspense of horror films

detailedExemplarData.title: How does cognitive psychology theories explain the enjoyment and suspense of horror films

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Topic is communicated accurately and consistently, with clear headings reflecting the investigationâs purpose
Research question is explicitly stated and revisited throughout, providing a coherent thread
Methodology draws on a range of scholarly sources, showing informed selection
weaknesses: Occasional vagueness in phrasing (e.g., âWhat is horror?â section drifting into definitions)
Research question remains somewhat broad and grammatically flawed, diluting focus
Methodology lacks explicit search strategy and relies on some less rigorous web sources
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs work consistently communicates the topic and purpose (band 5-6) but with minor clarity issues, meriting a top-end mid-band mark of 5 reduced to 4 to reflect occasional vagueness. The research question is clearly stated but only partially focused (band 3-4), justifying a 3. The methodology is mostly complete with relevant sources (band 3-4) but lacks explicit strategy, also meriting a 3. Averaging these strands yields a holistic score of 4 for Focus and Method.

strengths: Demonstrates solid foundational knowledge of cognitive theories (e.g., excitation-transfer, sensation seeking)
Generally correct application of subject-specific terminology and concepts
weaknesses: Several conceptual inaccuracies (e.g., oversimplifying cognitive theory vs. emotion)
Overreliance on non-peer-reviewed web sources for definitions
Use of terminology is occasionally imprecise and inconsistent
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student shows good baseline understanding and mostly accurate terminology (band 3-4), but conceptual errors and uneven source application limit performance to the lower half of that band, warranting a score of 3.

strengths: Research draws on a broad range of peer-reviewed sources directly relevant to horror, cognition, and emotion
Analysis moves beyond summary, questioning theoretical limitations and comparing perspectives
A coherent line of argument is sustained, acknowledging competing explanations
weaknesses: Depth of analysis is uneven; some conclusions lack sufficient evidence
Evaluation is present but not consistently critical, especially in the conclusion
Overreliance on a limited evidence base (e.g., single cartoon study) weakens generalizability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place research quality in the 4-6 band (score 5), analysis in 4-6 (score 4), and evaluation in 4-6 (score 4). Collectively these reflect solid but not exemplary critical thinking. Placing the overall score in the 7-9 band (upperâmid band) acknowledges strong relevance and reasoning while noting depth limitations, yielding a justified score of 7.

strengths: Clear, logical structure with appropriate use of headings and standard EE conventions
Title page, table of contents, and reference list are correctly formatted
Headings in the body and ToC mirror each other, aiding navigation
weaknesses: Table of contents lacks accurate page numbers
Inconsistent citation formatting and occasional typographical errors
Absent or glitchy pagination and layout artifacts reduce professionalism
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure aligns with conventions (band 3-4), while layout and formatting show notable inconsistencies (band 1-2). Holistically, this places Presentation in the mid-low band, meriting a score of 3.

strandSelections.reasoning: Subject-specific terms such as âhedonic compensatory solution,â âillusion theory,â âsensation seekingâ and âgender socialisationâ are employed, showing familiarity with disciplinary vocabulary.  However, usage is occasionally imprecise (e.g., plural âtheories explainâ), and definitions are sometimes partial.  Consistency is reasonable but not sustained at an excellent level, so the 3-4 band is most fitting; a 3 reflects largely accurate but not fully consistent terminology.

Basic layout elementsâtitle, word count, headings and a reference listâare present, indicating some attention to formatting.  Nevertheless, inconsistencies in citation style, absent page numbers in the extract, and occasional formatting glitches suggest that not all conventions are correctly applied.  This positions the work in the 1-2 band, with a score of 2 acknowledging partial but imperfect compliance.

The student demonstrates solid foundational knowledge of key psychological constructs (cognitive appraisal, excitation-transfer, sensation seeking) and applies them with generally correct explanations.  Nevertheless, several conceptual inaccuracies occur (e.g., equating cognitive theory with ânot real emotionsâ) and some claims rely on secondary commentary rather than primary empirical work.  Application of sources is usually relevant but sometimes merely descriptive.  This aligns with the middle band (3-4); a score of 3 recognises adequate but not advanced understanding.

The methodology is literature-based, drawing on journal articles, books and a few less scholarly web sources.  Citations such as Adolphs (2012) and Zillmann et al. (1986) show some informed selection, yet the inclusion of blogposts (e.g., FilmTheory.org) and inconsistent referencing weaken rigour.  No explicit explanation of search strategy or source evaluation is given.  This represents a mostly complete but not fully coherent methodology, matching the 3-4 band; 3 reflects the evident but moderate level of appropriateness.

The studentâs topic â the cognitive psychology behind the enjoyment and suspense of horror films â is introduced early, repeated in headings and woven through every major section.  The purpose (to examine why audiences enjoy and feel suspense in horror) is consistently referenced, and sub-sections such as âTheories of Fear Responseâ and âFactors Contributing to Horror Enjoymentâ align clearly with that purpose.  Minor language errors do not impede understanding.  

Overall the topic is communicated accurately and kept in view, matching the descriptor for the 5-6 band, though occasional vagueness in phrasing (e.g., âWhat is horror?â section drifting into definitional issues) prevents a flawless execution, so a mid-band score of 5 is appropriate.

Roughly twenty sources, many peer-reviewed, are cited and mostly pertain directly to horror, fear and cognition, indicating clear relevance.  Some sources (blog entries, popular articles) are of lower academic weight, and there is no primary data, but the breadth across psychology, aesthetics and media studies demonstrates good research quality overall.  These features fit solidly within the 4-6 band; a mid-band score of 5 reflects the mixture of strong and weaker selections.

The research question is stated explicitly (âHow does cognitive psychology theories explain the enjoyment and suspense of horror filmsâ) and is returned to in conclusion; however, the grammar is flawed and the scope remains broad, covering multiple constructs (enjoyment, suspense, diverse personality variables).  The discussion sometimes drifts into sociological or philosophical territory, indicating only partial focus.  These characteristics align with a clearly stated but only partially focused question, fitting the 3-4 band; a score of 3 reflects the lower end of that band.

The essay follows EE conventions: title page, abstract substitute, table of contents, numbered thematic sections and a reference list.  Headings are logical and aid navigation, though some pagination cues in the ToC are missing and several typographical artefacts (e.g., overlined numbers) interrupt flow.  Overall structure is clearly appropriate but not flawless, matching the 3-4 band; 3 is awarded to reflect minor weaknesses.

A line of argument is maintainedâcognitive processes mediate fear and enjoymentâand competing explanations are acknowledged.  Evaluation (e.g., noting flaws in Zillmannâs model) shows some critical stance.  However, discussion sometimes lacks depth, and the conclusion reiterates rather than synthesises insights.  The argument is reasoned but not fully developed, fitting the 4-6 band; a score of 4 reflects the lower-mid level within that range.

The essay moves beyond summary by questioning each theoryâs limitations (e.g., critiques of hedonic compensation and excitation-transfer).  Still, much of the writing reiterates study findings without deeper synthesis, and logical connections sometimes leap without sufficient evidence.  Conclusions are partly supported but not rigorously traced to data.  This represents clear but only moderately supported analysis, warranting placement in the 4-6 band at a score of 4.

annotations.comment: Table of contents is missing page numbers for each section, impairing navigation. Ensure all entries include accurate pagination.
Section headings in the ToC are logically ordered and mirror the body structure, aiding reader orientation.
The summary reiterates theories without explicitly linking back to the research question. Strengthen connections to demonstrate focus alignment.
Reference list contains inconsistent formatting and incomplete retrieval details (broken URLs, missing publication info). Standardize to a recognized style guide.
The title âThe cognitive psychology behind horror filmsâ is clearly stated at the start, fulfilling the requirement and providing immediate focus for the investigation.

The research question lacks grammatical precision and remains overly broad, spanning enjoyment, suspense and various personality variables. Consider narrowing scope and refining phrasing to strengthen focus.
Word count is provided and remains under the 4 000 limit, meeting formatting requirements.
Multiple spelling and typographical errors (e.g., âdisqustâ) reduce clarity and professionalism. Proofread carefully to enhance readability.
The opening paragraph lists behavioral effects but lacks integration with cognitive constructs. Introduce cognitive theories earlier to better frame the investigation.
The statement of purpose in the introduction revisits the research question but could more clearly define key terms such as âenjoymentâ and âsuspense.â
Definitions of horror rely on non-peer-reviewed web sources (e.g., Ceirus 2015). Prioritize primary academic literature to strengthen theoretical grounding.
The transition from defining âhorrorâ to focusing on cognitive processes is abrupt. Add a bridging paragraph to maintain coherence.
The explanation of cognitive theory mischaracterizes Walton (1978). Clarify that suspension of disbelief is voluntary and cues emotional engagement, not mere pretense.
Spelling of âvagueâ is incorrect (âvaqueâ). Address minor language errors to improve professionalism.
The assertion that horror âdoes not horrifyâ oversimplifies audience reactions. Incorporate empirical studies on physiological arousal to support or challenge this claim.
Discussion of the Thought Theory is descriptive but lacks critique of its explanatory power compared to illusion theory. Provide comparative evaluation.
The hedonic compensatory solution is described thoroughly but lacks discussion of empirical support and counter-examples. Integrate studies that test its predictions.
Analysis of the hedonic compensatory theoryâs limits is detailed, but could be strengthened by quantitative comparison or visualization of opposing data.
In the discussion of excitation transfer, the citation âZillmannâs, 1980â is inconsistent. Standardize referencing style and include full details.
The link between suspense, resolution and enjoyment is articulated, but overreliance on a single cartoon study weakens generalizability. Diversify evidence base.
The review of individual differences cites multiple studies but reads as a list. Synthesize findings to develop thematic insights about personality impacts.
The description of sensation seeking effects ends abruptly and lacks a concluding sentence. Ensure complete arguments for each trait discussed.
Integration of gender socialization theory is insightful, but the link to current social norms is speculative. Support claims with recent empirical studies or surveys.
The Conclusion heading is clearly marked, signposting the final synthesis effectively.
The conclusion restates key theories but largely repeats earlier content. Aim for final evaluative insights or proposed future research to enhance depth.
Duplicate and fragmented entries (e.g., Gaut 1993 vs. Gaut 2013) create confusion. Consolidate multiple listings of the same source and verify completeness.
An incomplete reference entry (âTurner, P. (2020â¦)â) lacks retrieval details. Verify and complete all bibliographic records.
